sections.heading: Plastic Mold Design
sections.text: Plastic Mold Design When designing a plastic mold, some factors should be taken to reduce the cost and complexity of parts. Design Rules Undercuts: reduce as many undercuts as possible, including external and internal undercuts, as well as side-action directions. A trick for removing the need for additional slides is creating a pass-thru core. Wall Thickness: maintain an even wall thickness to prevent wall sink, thinner walls can reduce cycle time and cost. Drafts: the standard draft angle is about 1° to 2°. parts can be designed with a minimum draft angle of 0.5°, or up to 5°. sections.heading: How to Make Injection Mold
sections.text: How to Make Injection Mold Injection mold is a tool used for producing plastic parts by the injection molding process, it gives the molded components complete structure and precise dimensions. How to make or manufacture injection mold? 1) Analysis of plastic parts Before the mold design, the designer should fully analyze whether the plastic products conform to the injection molding processing principle. It includes necessary discussion on the geometric shape, dimensional accuracy, and appearance requirements of the products to avoid unnecessary complexity in mold manufacturing. 2) Mold design A set of high-quality molds not only needs good equipment and skilled mold-making workers but also...

sections.heading: What Is Aluminum Mold?
sections.text: What Is Aluminum Mold? Aluminum molds are tools used in the injection molding process, they can be manufactured from different types of aluminum alloys, including 7075, 6061, 2024, etc. The aluminum tooling is known for its high heat dissipation rate, which brings lots of benefits for manufacturing and components. Aluminum molds are generally made by CNC machining, and the softness of the aluminum material allows for fast machining. Aluminum injection molds can be applied to more than 100 different materials, including thermoplastics and thermosets, such as ABS, PC, PP, POM, and more. Benefits of Aluminum Molds ✅ Lightweight & Cost-Efficient Up to 50 % lighter than steel, reducing lifting e...

Benefits of Aluminum Molds ✅ Lightweight & Cost-Efficient Up to 50 % lighter than steel, reducing lifting e...

sections.heading: ✅ Lightweight & Cost-Efficient
sections.text: ✅ Lightweight & Cost-Efficient Up to 50 % lighter than steel, reducing lifting equipment needs and installation costs. Lower material prices also cut overall mold-making expenses.

sections.heading: ✅ Faster Cycle Times
sections.text: ✅ Faster Cycle Times High thermal conductivity enables rapid heating & cooling, shortening cycle time and lowering energy use while minimizing sink marks and voids.

sections.heading: ✅ Excellent Machinability
sections.text: ✅ Excellent Machinability Easy machining yields smoother surfaces and shorter production lead-times—days versus weeks for steel—ideal for prototyping and low-volume runs.

Benefits of Aluminum Molds ✅ Lightweight & Cost-Efficient Up to 50 % lighter than steel, reducing lifting e...

sections.heading: ? Production Volume
sections.text: ? Production Volume Steel excels beyond 1 million parts; aluminum suits 50 k–100 k cycles, making it perfect for small-batch production and prototyping.

sections.heading: ? Cost Consideration
sections.text: ? Cost Consideration Aluminum has lower upfront material and fabrication costs, ideal when budget constraints are paramount. Steel’s higher initial cost is offset over long runs.

sections.heading: ? Durability & Longevity
sections.text: ? Durability & Longevity Steel withstands far more cycles with minimal wear. Some aluminum molds may require replacement after ~10 k shots, reducing cost-effectiveness for long runs.

sections.heading: ? Design Features & Tolerances
sections.text: ? Design Features & Tolerances Steel’s hardness ensures tighter tolerances and sharper fine features. Aluminum is softer, making it less suited for ultra-precise details but adequate for many applications.

sections.heading: ? Ease of Repair & Modification
sections.text: ? Ease of Repair & Modification Aluminum can be repaired or modified quickly and economically; steel’s hardness often necessitates complete replacement after deformation.

sections.heading: Injection Molding Processes – Types of Injection Molding
sections.text: Injection Molding Processes – Types of Injection Molding From sketch to functional parts, our injection molding services can help you get the final products through multiple molding methods based on applications, materials, sizes, and more factors. Custom plastic injection molding A plastic manufacturing process using polymers or plastic resins after being heated, melted and injected under high pressure into an injection mold to obtain molded parts with the desired shape. Liquid Silicone Rubber Molding The injection molding of liquid silicon rubber (LSR) to manufacture pliable and durable parts, the process involves mixing together a two-component compound, then heat-cure it in the mold wit...

sections.heading: What is Injection Molding?
sections.text: What is Injection Molding? Injection molding (moulding) is the most commonly used manufacturing process for manufacturing plastic parts. It refers to the method that under a certain temperature, the plastic material completely melted by screw stirring is injected into the mold cavity with high pressure, and then cooled and solidified to obtain the injection molded product. This method is really cost-effective and it’s suitable for the mass production of complex shape parts. Molding also has the highest variety of materials, colors, and configurations when compared to CNC machining or even 3D printing. Beyond materials, injection molded parts can have custom cosmetics, polishes, or surface t...

sections.heading: Secondary Operations
sections.text: Secondary Operations Laser Engraving the process of utilizing a laser to etch a picture or logo onto a piece of material of your choice. Heat Staking the process of selectively heating a plastic component in order to reconstruct it and insert another component, such as a threaded insert.Pad printing is when an ink-based, two-dimensional design is applied to a three-dimensional surface. Painting adds colored coatings to molded parts, including coatings for top and bottom, standard colors or Pantone colors, and more.
